title: Do i need to code and register battery ? - Page 2
body_text: Just thought I'd share a nice tutorial video I ran into on YouTube regarding installation of the more recent versions of ISTA+* and ISTA-P** * ISTA(+) : Used as ISTA-D for E series, and ISTA-D AND P for all later models ** ISTA-P (e.g. v.3.71) : Used as "programming tool"(ISTA-P) for E series as ISTA(+), since late 2017/early 2018, does not include programming features for E series). "Installing and Configuring ISTAD and ISTA-P for K+DCAN Connections. Easy Step by Step Instructions" The video above describes a slightly older version of the software than the latest, but the install process is pretty much the same. The only difference you may encounter(as I did) is that among the recent ISTA software there are ones which do NOT require a license (like the torrent version of ISTA 4.39.20 I got). ISTA+ with just the diagnostic data, only BLP data(required) installed and no SDP prorgramming data(optional, usually the 100GB+ largest PSdZ file in downloads), on a Windows 10 VM optimized for virtual disk space came out to about 116GB. This is including all diagnostic data for E series and up. *Update: I thought, that if the other series' data(BLP data) are filtered out, it might be possible to reduce the total size down, but it seems that the largest chuck of data(74~80GB) is due to the SQLiteDBs (Other & English basic files that are required), and the BLP data is just under 14GB so deleting some of those will still not make a huge dent in the size of the overall system. ISTA(+)'s E series info seems pretty much the same black & white photos with same text instructions at least for the same info I referenced compared with the older version from around 2016. But the ISTA graphic user interface is a bit upgraded / expanded, for example with capacity to handle 3 vehicles in one ISTA system for diagnostics, though likely not 3 vehicles at once for programming, and also some minor convenience features added to the main search list/viewing windows such as arrow heads to expand left or right windows fully left or right. Loading(booting) seems a bit faster but searches seem to take a bit longer - of course that just might be because my computer(2011 MacBook Air) is now "lacking" compared with ISTA's hardware requirements (just barely managing to run the software). In terms of diagnostic capability, there is likely not that much advantage to the latest ISTA+ compared with say ISTA-D 3.5X from around 2016-17, whereas in the older versions it is possible to delete primary data for models(series) not in use, allowing for significant reduction in system(VM) size (my older ISTA-D 3.5X VMs with only "E89"(E series) data are as small as 50~60GB. *Note: regarding hardware requirements, ISTA+ has a 150GB hard disk / 3.8GB RAM minimum requirement. These values are the minimum that need to be showing in the C: drive properties for example. In my case, my VM's size was showing as 149GB(though my physical drive was actually less available!) and ISTA+ initially aborted installation, so I had to expand my VM's potential maximum disk size, with some elbow room to 160GB and also increase the RAM setting to a little over 3.8GB (of 4GB max on my hardware system) to get ISTA+ to complete installation and start up. I think there is actually a registry tweak to disable this hardware requirement check at start up, but I did not have it handy so I just boosted my VM's capacity (virtually). My hardware ("host") system still managed probably because I have a SSD 'hard drive' and 10GB or so has been set as virtual memory on top of the 4GB hardware RAM(3GB or so of which was being consistently hogged by the ISTA VM "guest" system). ISTA-P, the latest v.3.71, I've downloaded but not yet installed (as I'd first like to optimize the ISTA+ VM a bit, but it(ISTA-P 3.71) should be a bit simpler in get up and running, thanks to this video guide with detailed step by step instruction in clear English. This will significantly reduce if not eliminate the need to search forums for trouble shooting tips etc.. Cheers~

title: Do i need to code and register battery ? - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by StradaRedlands With the knowledge that there is a history recorded, I took a chance that the Foxwell would be able to read it, and yes, it's there! Short Answer: You're Good to Go! Details: The CRITICAL thing is NOT whether or not the km/mileage of "Last Battery Replacement" was entered. What IS Critical is that the battery TYPE & CAPACITY are correctly selected.  THAT is NOT done automatically when "Battery Registration" occurs, but is a totally different procedure. YOUR Foxwell Screen Image shows Current Battery Capacity Setting: "AGM" Type "105 Ah" Capacity Your Interstate Replacement Battery is AGM, 100 Ah Capacity. The two available AGM settings nearest that capacity are 90 Ah & 105 Ah, so the installer correctly selected/ retained the 105 Ah AGM setting.  Since the km at replacement (110,880 km) is consistent with mileage in 2/2023, then "Registration" occurred at that mileage.  When the "Battery Registration" button is pressed on whatever Tool is used to "Register" the battery, the DME Power Management Module simple enters the current mileage (110,880 km in your case) in a text field. THAT has NO impact on Type/Capacity setting. There is another function of battery registration that MOST Tools perform: The "Histogram" or record of SoC (State of Charge) and SoH (State of Health) of the battery is CLEARED/ Reset, so that the data that appears in the Histogram ONLY reflects battery condition since new battery was installed. Although it is preferable to Reset Histogram when installing Replacement Battery, it is NOT critical (NOR is entering mileage/km ;-), as the Last 5 days are the only thing in the Histogram the Power Management looks at to charge battery, and those "last 5 days (tagen) are OVER-WRITTEN in 5 days, so a week later, there is absolutely NO consequence to NOT having Reset Histogram.  You just have OLD data in the Histogram that does NOT relate to new battery, and most folks either don't know there IS a Histogram, or don't know how to use it. So you can see how CONFUSED many folks are about "Battery Registration". George

title: Do i need to code and register battery ? - Page 2
body_text: Bump. This is the most useful thread on the subject so far, thanks to gbalthrop and his wealth of knowledge. Here is how I understand the situation: 1. Coding a new battery - tells the control units what size and type of battery are present, for advanced electronic system functions and diagnostics 2. Registering a new battery - tells the DME/ECM (in my case MSV70) if the battery is new or old. This allows the DME/ECM to control the charging from alternator more closely, in conjunction with the IBS. I'm pretty sure the battery registration only has to do with the DME, since post #6 on M3Post only mentions the DME when dealing with the IBS: https://www.m3post.com/forums/showthread.php?t=1852097 In my case, I checked the VO for the CAS and saw the battery as B070 - lead acid with 70 Ah capacity. Funny enough, I'd been driving with an AGM in there for nearly 7 years, replaced by previous owner. He told me he 'coded' the battery to the car, but I actually think the battery was simply registered to the car. After 105k KM and close to 7y, that battery died, so i replaced it with a lead acid 70ah battery. I was successfully able to register my new battery using INPA, in the IBS menu and the B Change function. I did see the current ODO value update once I did this, so I assume the registration was a success. Interestingly enough, my IBS has been disconnected for the duration of my ownership period, and the car has run totally fine. Given that the battery was coded already to B070 and no IBS connected, I am going to leave the system as-is. From this thread, it would seem that the charging system will output a default voltage (read somewhere 14.2V?). I doubt this value will overcharge the battery in the long-term, and I only plan to keep the car another couple of years (until then next $1000 bill comes). I guess the only thing that might be an issue for me, since I leave the car sitting for expended periods of time and drive work trucks, is that when stationary there will be no IBS monitoring for SOC.

title: Do i need to code and register battery ? - Page 2
body_text: Hello Instead of making new post i will ask on this. I have to change my battery. I got one from my friend that is used and is only 1-2 years old. That battery is 96ah agm. My currently original bmw battery is 90ah agm.(15 years old). Should i just register the battery as ”same” capacity? Or register is as higher capacity? Do i need also to code it? Its just 6ah difference and also its used so capacity right now is probably less then 96ah. What would you do? Thank you in advance.

That battery is 96ah agm. My currently original bmw battery is 90ah agm.(15 years old). Should i just register the battery as ”same” capacity? Or register is as higher capacity? Do i need also to code it? Its just 6ah difference and also its used so capacity right now is probably less then 96ah. What would you do? Thank you in advance. Close enough IIRC... default to the lower setting if you're in between. So you don't need to code in a higher amp battery, just register it as new so the charging level changes.

title: How many original owners left?
body_text: Just curious as to how many of us are still here. EfthreeOh finally dropped off the original owner rolls, but certainly holds the mileage record! And the deer kill record probably. I still have my RWD 6spd '11 e91 that will be 15 in July, hopefully has spent it's last winter chilling in the garage in Maine as I am building a new house in Florida with a big garage. Once that happens, the car is finally moving south to join my 128i down here. Looking forward to putting the original German license plate back on the front too! 56530 miles as of last October. IIRC there are a couple folks on here who have fewer miles than I do who bought them new. Still only "repair" is a new battery since the warranty expired. I had the rear bumper and plastic hatch top repainted after one of my idiot housemates dragged a box across the top of the car, and it needed a new lower grill piece after I hit a bird at speed that punched a bird-shaped hole in it. I probably should get new tires due to age before driving it 1700 miles south, and it needs to visit the PDR spa for a couple of minor door dings. And due for a full fluids service. No leaks (at least as of last October), still a delight to drive. Has had the BMW Performance Intake and Exhaust since re-delivery. Doesn't really make it go any faster, but it sure sounds faster. At the Berlin Wall two days after delivery: DSCN0490 by kevinr1916 , on Flickr Shame it's not possible to do either Euro Delivery or buy a wagon if you are in NA anymore.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today?
body_text: I spend too much time at my local wrecker, so thought I would start documenting it! Sometimes there are real scores, other days I walk out empty handed. I know there are a few more of you out there as well, so post up some finds! Today I got lucky and noticed an E92 335i that came to my local lot yesterday. I went down this morning, and was pretty successful scavenging off of it. It ended up being a bonafide M Sport that didn't smell like tobacco/weed and was overall pretty well kept. Best of all it had an unmolested complete glacier silver aluminum trim, although it was pretty scratched up. I didn't notice it till I got there but it also had the thicker steering wheel with paddle shifters but with the top leather in rough condition. Door sill trim and the headliner were already pulled, but I got the dead pedal as well. Ended up breaking the tabs on that with an overly-aggressive yank. I contemplated the wheels, which were a full set but painted black and in otherwise decent condition. Not sure why someone went to the effort of pulling the headliner and pillar trim but left the rest of the fancy parts. Anyway, here's the steering wheel and dead pedal: And the complete interior trim: It did kinda' suck today as the guy checking me out did everything by the book and charged me a by the foot price for the trim. He was training a new guy. Usually it's a veteran person and they just want me out of there so ring up a per item price vs. the very expensive by the foot price. I guess it's evened out over time so can't complain too much. I'll keep the steering wheel in case I ever get an automatic, put the dead pedal on my current car, and probably sell the interior trim off after I clean it up.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today?
body_text: Most LKQ prices are now comparable to eBay prices. eBay prices where you make a few clicks and have a part delivered to your door. I still head over once in a while if I need something asap and don’t want to deal with shipping times, but for most of the parts, it’s about 50/50. There is people who actually walk along the yards for a few hours looking for parts to resell and they make a living doing it. I am not a fan of some of them nit picking each part. I know it’s a business that has to make money, but I thought their 30% increase in prices was to avoid that and sell you a complete part with all internals attached. I remember buying a seat a few years ago and the side airbag was blown. I argued that seat airbag was no good to anyone and I could remove it at home, I just wanted to get out of there. She was persistent about charging me for it. I did have to remove it because there was no convincing that could be done to show that it was useless. Back to the title, last thing I bought was an airbag. $70 and took 20 minutes. Saved $60 and a few days shipping time. They used to have 20% off weekends and a points program. I would line up my points with the discount weekends and walk in with 60% off. My complete (heated, sport, rear folding seats, rear sunshades) terra brown e90 interior ran me $120 in those days.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today?
body_text: I went to my local wrecker yard looking for a few miscellaneous parts and left happy. There were two very rare junk yard finds: 2008 E92 335xi 2007 E93 335i The E92 has the red leather interior which was complete and in decent shape, definitely restorable, however they were non-sport seats. The E93 had beige leather but again it wasn't the sport seats. That car also had the trunk lid and entire roof assembly taken. I always told myself if I ever found a E93 in the yard I'd stock up on whatever parts I could get, but I didn't actually find anything worth grabbing as a spare. Maybe the hydraulic pump, but there are plenty of good repair services if mine ever fails. The various roof sensors are relatively inexpensive new, so I didn't bother with those either. That E93 had what looked like very mild front end damage so I'm surprised it was in the pick a part yard, though the engine looked nasty with an oil filter housing leak that was neglected. There was a 2011 LCI 328i too which had a few decent parts but nothing from the interior. There was dried blood spray all over. I am always on the lookout for N54 injectors. My last trip to the junkyard I found a bunch but no index -12s and all the ones I pulled from two or three cars were dirty and soaked in oil. This time all the N54s there had all the injectors taken.  Hope they don't end up on the secondary markets as counterfeit -12s. I did manage to get what I went for, and the prices were better than expected. Got the latest version of the CCC unit for my niece's 325i. The original unit on that car finally died with the typical boot loop. I know I could always try a reflow or even upgrade the whole thing to CIC, but I got the latest CCC system and the plastic bezel for $55 so that's a real easy replacement, just hope it works. I also got a N52 injector for that car, a cupholder trim piece for my car, an EKPM3 module, and a side mirror for my 330.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today? - Page 2
body_text: Saturday I went to the local pick yard. Strangely enough they had 2, black 325i's on the yard. No other e90's at all. It was good for me as I needed black 325i parts. My sons car was gotten cheap, and some of the reason was scratches/dents on the driver and passenger doors, and a trunk lid with very bad/peeling paint. Obviously a bad respray. Also the left side speakers quit working a couple weeks ago and the right rear tire kept loosing air, ever since I put new tires on in September. Tire shop corrected for free last week but mentioned due to corrosion on the wheel bead it was just a matter of time before it started to leak again. So Saturday I grabbed a front and rear passenger door, 1 each from 2 different cars, trunk lid, Hifi amp and a spare matching wheel. Installed the amp yesterday and that fixed the stereo issue. Today my son and I installed the trunk lid and doors. Getting the trunk to line up was very fiddly. Seemed when the body lines were correct the striker was not and vice-versa. Eventually we got it lined up correctly and closing properly. The doors were much easier. Still had to shim the rear door a good bit for alignment. (glad I grabbed the shims at the junk yard in addition to the ones from the existing doors). All locks and windows work properly. Even got lucky with the passenger door mirror being the same as what was on there so it functions properly. We still need to swap the interior panels from the old doors as they are black and the replacements are gray. Also the existing doors windows are a different shade of tint than the new ones so we will swap the glass over as well. Project for tomorrow. It wasn't until I started cleaning a spot on the new rear door I realized we have an issue. Our car is Schwartz. I am a bit colorblind mind you but the new door is black. It's also not just black. Diamond Schwartz. It was super dark and overcast on Saturday plus the normal dirty salvage yard coating of grime and I truly didn't notice a color difference. Even my wife and son didn't see it until there was good lighting and a clean spot. Oh well. I am sure it will throw people off. Hope it doesn't cause any wrecks along the way.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today? - Page 2
body_text: All my local yards have converted into non self serve.  I so miss the days of good old pick and pulls.  Was even better when I was flying with USAF, We traveled through Germany twice a week and Both Spangdahlem and Ramstein have semi decent pick and pulls.  Ramstein's is a hike from Billeting but I would walk there and take a cab back.  It was the best from 99 to about 2010 but after that E30's started drying up and E36's in EU were never decent, they were normally M40 powered 316 or 318's with occasional M50/M5x but you would never see an S50b30 or S54.

title: What Parts Did You Pick Today? - Page 2
body_text: Pretty solid score today at the local! Yesterday both an E70 with a hitch, and an E90 with an alarm popped up on the inventory. This morning I'm there bright and early with some tools! Both vehicles were still intact, so I was able to get the hitch (aftermarket, no wiring harness) as well as the complete alarm package for $110 and a hour of my time! I was hoping to get a spare wheel and the cargo floor strut from the E70, but it ended up being a three row vehicle. I did a quick check of some E60s to see if I can pull an oil cooler real quick, but the engines were all smashed into the radiators. They cut away the front subframe on all these cars in order to pull the cats, so it's hard to get access to anything in the front of the engine bay. But I'm really happy about the hitch as I want to get my bike rack up and running. Alarm is just bonus for the inevitable next car!

body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by E93Seattle (Racerbruce) well done strada! the blue bomber is destined for one of your girls, correct? any thoughts on the next acquisition?? It will definitely end up with one of the kids, but I don't know which one! Right now they all kind of share it based on need. The Baby has driven it down to a track meet in Murrieta today, but First Born likes driving it out to college classes in Riverside. If Middle Child can get a third year on campus (lottery based), she won't need a car, but if she has to live off campus, who knows what her needs will be. Anyway, long/short of that is we'll probably need it another car at some point in the future. I wouldn't mind another e91 for them as they're such great do-everything cars for active people. But I also have the itch for an e93, so if I come across the perfect one for me, maybe one of them can inherit my 330i. The only downside is that car is definitely getting up in mileage, and I'm not sure what the reliability will be like. Also my wife's F10 has always kind of annoyed me... continual little problems keep popping up, and they're all about 20% more expensive to fix than on an e90! I'm kind of kicking around the idea of replacing it with a X1 35i. They're basically lifted e91s with the N55 motor, so seem kind of kick ass! Here's one that ticks off a lot of the boxes that is a good example of what I'd like . But she loves tat F10, so would be a tough sell. TMI? Well, you asked...

title: Replaced Battery Car Stalled, Does Not Start
body_text: After replacing the battery, while driving home the car died and will no start.  The car was running fine for about 2 weeks.  The car cranks but does not start. Before the battery I did replace the fuel pump. I thought the EKPM went bad when the car died. Now the car does not see the EKPM in INPA. When I go to NCS Expert I get an error stating the VIN is faulty. I have tried a new EKMP3 and old is a EKPM2. I have verified voltage and at the EKPM and canbus voltages EKPM pin 1 12v pin 9 2.72v Pin 16 2.32v I am pretty new to INPA and NCS expert.  I have attached a few screenshots of INPA.  This was basically just me playing around INPA and trying to figure things out. Any help with this would be great! Its been pretty easy keeping the car running up to this point. This problem has been a huge pain!

raw_payload.author: whackedbear
raw_payload.subforum_name: General E90 / E91 / E92 / E93 Discussion

metadata_json.subforum_name: General E90 / E91 / E92 / E93 Discussion
metadata_json.author: whackedbear

title: Replaced Battery Car Stalled, Does Not Start
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by whackedbear After replacing the battery, while driving home the car died and will no start.  The car was running fine for about 2 weeks.  The car cranks but does not start. Before the battery I did replace the fuel pump. I thought the EKPM went bad when the car died. Now the car does not see the EKPM in INPA. [It SHOULD appear as "17 EKP" between 12 DME & 18 EGS, on your INPA > Functional Jobs > F2 Identification screen. See Below] I have tried a new EKMP3 and old is a EKPM2. I have verified voltage and at the EKPM and canbus voltages EKPM: pin 1 12v [COLOR="red"][X13663/1?][/COLO pin 9 2.72v [X13663/9?] Pin 16 2.32v [X13663/16?] [Continuity to Chassis Ground at X13663/2, Brown wire?] ... Good Data, but some questions on the timeline: 1) When (date) was Battery Replaced? Car started & ran after Battery replacement? 2) When was Fuel Pump Replaced? Car started & ran AFTER fuel pump replacement? 3) When was EKPS (Fuel Pump Module) replaced? Did car START & Run BEFORE replacement? AFTER replacement? That was replaced AFTER "Crank, NO Start" issue occurred? 4) What components had NOT yet been replaced (EKPS?) when your ISTA ScreenPrints were saved yesterday, 12/17/2025, at approximately 3:30 PM, your time. 5) Did you ever (earlier) use INPA > Functional Jobs > F2, Identification to View/Save a List of ALL Modules, & was "17 EKPS" there? If so, what was the "SGBD" or Variant of that module, per the 4th column of the Screen? Since you did NOT provide the Last-7 Characters of your VIN , I GUESS you have an N52 (since you're in MI), 2008 or Later 328xi E92, since your modules predominantly have 2008 or later software revision dates. If that is WRONG, please advise so I can refer to correct ISTA SSP Wiring Diagram. Actually, if you have a JBBF87 Variant/SGBD JBBF, you probably have an earlier model. Also, if your F2, Identification screen is only showing 9 Modules (NO  01 MRS, 12 DME & others), then your COM 1 settings have probably been reset by Windows Updates, or you need to hit F2 repeatedly. As you appear to know, when a Module (here EKPS) does NOT appear on the INPA > Functional Jobs > F2, Identificaton screen (OR the ISTA Control Unit Tree/List), that is usually because: 1) there is NO power supply to the Module, here NO voltage via KL30G, Accessory Relay, Ignition ON, X13663/1, Red/Black wire; 2) there is NO Bus communication via (here) PT-CAN, X13663/9 & X13663/16; your voltages appear normal; 3) there is NO Continuity to Chassis Ground at X13663/2, Brown Wire, to complete the circuit for power supply; 4) there is a fault in the Module (here EKPS). In your case, you appear to have tested everything but Chassis Ground at X13663/2. You can also "Hotwire" the fuel pump by applying voltage (from X13663/1) to X3507/4 (Red/White wire) & Chassis Ground to X3507/2 (Brown wire). Do NOT Open the Fuel tank -- Risk of Spark/Explosion of fuel vapor!! Attached is ISTA ScreenPrint of fuel pump circuit. 2008 & Later, &  Early 2007 Models are SAME except for fuse# (F40 Early, F70 2008 & Later). George

In your case, you appear to have tested everything but Chassis Ground at X13663/2. You can also "Hotwire" the fuel pump by applying voltage (from X13663/1) to X3507/4 (Red/White wire) & Chassis Ground to X3507/2 (Brown wire). title: Replaced Battery Car Stalled, Does Not Start
body_text: Above I see CAS errors from INPA of A0B1, A0B2, A0B4, A10A, A0B5.  Are these not the right error codes? A0B1 (CAS: Input, Brake Lights Implausible)  Since car cranks I think the brake input works A0B2 (CAS: Supply, Terminal 30E/30L)  will double check fuses A0B4 (CAS: Engine Start, Starter Operation)  Car does crank A10A (CAS: Internal Module Fault)  should I consider the CAS bad and look into sending it in for repair? A0B5 (CAS: Road Speed Signal) I have replaced the fuel pump and there has been no change. Is there anything else I should check?

title: Replaced Battery Car Stalled, Does Not Start
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by whackedbear I don't see anywhere I can see the Fuel Pressure in INPA.  Is this because the car doesnt see the the EKPM and its not programed?  Ive been trying to find the screen to see the fuel pressure for about 20 mins.  It sounds like my fuel pump has not stopped running at all.  When I try to activate the Fuel Pump in INPA it stays on, when i deactivate it, it stays on.  I have tried the original EKPM2, a EKPM3 and a ARC EKPM3.  They all have the same behavior. I did find it in PROTOOL.  Fuel Pressure was showing @ 86PSI There is no fuel pressure sensor on an N51 or N52 engine, so you cannot read the pressure with INPA or Protool, or ISTA. It needs to be measured manually with an external pressure gauge. The value you see in PROTOOL is a ghost value.

title: What is the REST button?? 🤔
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by greenohawk69 Can be used to keep the interior warm in cold weather. Draws warm air from engine bay into the car. No. It uses the residual heat in the cooling system (i.e. hot coolant) heater circuit to run the cabin heating when the engine is turned off. It works because the E90 engine(s) use an electric coolant pump to circulate engine coolant. The REST function circulates hot coolant through the heater core after the engine is turned off and runs the HVAC blower motor to circulate air over the heater core. OP, the accessory mode of the ignition needs to be on (i.e. engine off) and the cabin temperature set to heat for the REST function to work, and the coolant needs to be hot. Also, if the battery is not sufficiently charged the REST function will not work. It will run about 18 minutes on a cold day and stops once the coolant temperature falls below a set threshold.  The owners manual explains how to use the REST function.

Once i found out the exact problem, i created a DIY 'fix-it' for people having issues in the future. This should take some hassle off of E92-lighting.com for sure ^__^ Quote: Originally Posted by mikekilpatrick Came home from a 10 day trip to Florida, got home from the airport, threw my bags in the house and then immediately went out to my car, vacuumed the whole car, while I ran it for a half hour or so with the windows open and then armor alled the whole car.  Then, took it out for a drive to brush the rust off the brakes and fill it up with premium.  AFTER that, I went home and unpacked...  Missed my Beamer while on vacation! ^__^ i completely understand missing your car... i leave to float around in the pacific for 3-4 months at a time, usually not even seeing land for a month and a half. Typically first thing i do when i get home from deployment is take the car out to about 150mph , come back, and sit at home and get hammered with the friends

title: What did you do to your car today? - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by malachi347 stealership just quoted me better part of $600 to replace my rear driver window regulator... Got the parts from the dealer / how much? Any tips / tricks needed or was it completely straightforward? 15 minutes, really? I paid a total of $110 but I'm getting reimbursed for it. The job is fairly easy. Check out this vid if you want to see how to do it. Tips: In the photo below, position the window so that the green clip is lined up with the hole you just above the motor. To remove the top-most torx screw from the window regulator motor, you're going to have remove the middle and bottom window regulator nuts and only loosen the top one. I didn't go behind the window like it's done in the video to unclip the green bracket from the window; I went at it from the front. PM me if you require additional information.

title: E91 Smaller sunroof panel stuck in back.

One of the supports for the front/bigger panel is also coming off. Is there a way I can fix this or should I bring it to my local BMW dealer to see if they can fix it

title: E91 Smaller sunroof panel stuck in back.
body_text: This is probably something you could troubleshoot yourself depending on your comfort level. For the rear panel, you could first drop the headliner in the rear and see if you can realign it. Just a bunch of screws and clips, not too hard. If that doesn’t work- and to possibly fix your front panel- you’d probably have to consider removing and resetting those panels completely. This would entail removing the front glass, similar to the process you’d have to go through if you were to reupholster the panels. There are a decent amount of YouTube videos on this.

title: Bulletproofing '09 328xi E92 - What has been done, what I plan to do, and tell me what I've missed
body_text: Howdy y'all, Recently got my hands on an '09 328xi manual and have been loving it. It does everything I need it to and is a ton of fun in the first 3 gears which is all I need. The car is in pretty solid shape and I have the service records from the previous two owners. The car currently sits at 188k km (116800 miles) and I want to make it its as reliable as I can make it for the long term. Things that were done by previous owners that others warn can be an issue: Water pump and thermostat were replaced at 150k km Undercarriage rust proofing (I live in northern Ontario so salt is a bane in the winter) Things I have checked: Weatherproofing seals around all windows are in great shape No rust (or very minimal) around all panel gaps and entry points Things I have done: Put the muffler back on cuz it was silly loud. I still have the resonator but it sounds good without it (previous owner did a muffler and resonator delete - Straight piped) Full fluid replace (except for the transmission) New pads and discs Things I plan to do: Mickey mouse flange as soon as the weather is good - already have it ready to go 3 stage intake manifold for just a bit more HP Repair paint chips and minor surface scuffs Identify slight rough idle (will clean vanos solenoids) as well as replace coils + sparkies if need be Tighten steering rack as there is a small dead zone for the steering wheel atm Address the intermittent ABS 4x4 warning that comes on once in a while (I think its a bad ABS sensor as the warning comes on and off. I have a code scanner that I'm going to use on my next drive to see what codes it throws) Anything I might have missed or things that others have found can begin to occur at this mileage? I also want to very much do preventative maintenance and replace/repair parts before they become a problem. Looking to ensure this lady stays here for the long term and can get me to and from long haul trips reliably.

title: Bulletproofing '09 328xi E92 - What has been done, what I plan to do, and tell me what I've missed
body_text: I have had two N52s.   Both needed a starter at around 125k miles, so perhaps do that pre-emptively when you do the three-stage work. I also needed coils and spark plugs at 100k miles.  FCP sells a kit for $300. One of mine needed the oil pressure sensor at 130k, but that only cost $6 and took 5 minutes to replace. Your coolant overflow canister should have sprung a leak by now.  Same for the coolant line that runs from the canister across the top of the radiator.  Both are easy fixes, so might as well replace those when you do the Mickey Mouse flange.

title: Bulletproofing '09 328xi E92 - What has been done, what I plan to do, and tell me what I've missed
body_text: Starter is kind of a toss up - normally I'd recommend against doing it preventatively because the parts quality is kind of bad these days and your original might be better than what you'd buy now.  I'd maybe have one on hand but not replace it until you need it?  For reference one of my N52 starters is at 211k on the original, the other one stopped at about 150k. On the other hand - since you do actually notice it struggle - maybe just go ahead and do it.  Be sure you get a new set of bolts for it, the starter bolts are aluminum.  And you'll need to remove the intake manifold, so have a set of intake manifold gaskets and a throttle body gasket on hand.  Maybe go ahead and do the starter while you're switching to the 3 stage manifold? You could also consider getting a crankshaft sensor (BMW or VDO only) and doing that along with the starter if you want to guarantee you don't have to go back in that area.  I think you need to order the new bolt for that separately also.  Heads up on that - I struggled for a while to get the new crankshaft sensor to go into the engine because the o-ring wouldn't fit.  I ended up very carefully putting the o-ring into the hole making sure not to drop it inside, and then put the sensor through the o-ring instead of putting it on the sensor and pushing that into the engine.  Went in much better that way.  Be sure it's completely seated first before you put the bolt in, don't use the bolt to squeeze the sensor down. I'm a huge fan of the Schley Tools sly-14550 hose release tools for getting those push connectors on the vacuum lines off, I haven't broken one since I got it. Coolant vent hose and mickey mouse flange are the two big cooling system items...the other radiator hoses usually hold up much better.  Is the mickey mouse flange you have OE or aftermarket?  If it's aftermarket it's worth getting the BMW o-ring, if you don't have it already, because the aftermarket flanges themselves are fine but the o-rings tend to leak.  Expansion tank is also probably getting old just from age.  Unfortunately the only good ones are the OE BMW ones.  I took mine out for something else a while back and while I had it out I looked inside and the plastic was pretty brown and worn out.  It wasn't leaking but I don't think it had a ton of time left. Any leaks from the OFHG or valve cover? Have the belt and tensioner been replaced?  That's probably the other big one...make sure the belt isn't starting to slip off the tensioner.  Another overlooked item is the voltage regulator and pulley on the alternator...I like to do those preventatively. I wouldn't bother with the oil pressure switch myself...failures are rare but if it happens just do it then.

Yeah I know aftermarket starters can have quality issues, I'll see if maybe I can have mine rebuilt? I'll do some digging but if I'm going to do the intake manifold anyways I might as well. And I didn't know about the bolts, I'll get some solid replacements. Also with the crankshaft sensor I'll do that too and I appreciate the o-ring tribulations, I'll keep this thread handy and grab the tool you suggest when I do it so that I don't struggle as much. The mickey mouse flange I have is just a generic aftermarket aluminum one but I was planning on getting a proper o-ring for it since I don't trust the cheap rubber/plastic that comes with those cheap parts. I'll take a look at my expansion tank too. Unsure about the OFHG or valve cover atm, I'll take a look once the weather warms up again. And I believe so, I'll need to check the service records again but I think that was another warning I saw during my research but I'll double check too. I just remembered that the head gasket was also replaced around 100k km so I'm good on that as well. I'll take a look at the voltage regulator as well and keep an eye on the oil pressure switch.

body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by kkasson Actually I'm gonna change my mind on what I said above about the starter - I was double checking records and my 211k is not original, it was actually changed at 120k before I bought the car.  So based on that I'd say yeah go ahead and replace it around 100 or 120k to be safe.  The SEG one is good, and some people on the forum like the TYC one from RockAuto but I don't have any experience with that. The TYC starter has been great for me and others here too. Cranks faster as well. I used the peel back the intake method to save time and not break intake connections. You need to pull both the intake fasteners and studs and there's then just room to get in there. I also used a HF swiveling socket wrench to get at that pesky rear starter bolt. Also, change the intake gaskets IMHO.

title: New here, new E90 - Page 2
body_text: Eibach lowering springs and Sachs sport shocks. Used to have B8 dampers paired with the Eibach springs, but had issues with corrosion of the chrome on the piston of the damper. Here is the thread that documents my experience: https://www.e90post.com/forums/showt...php?p=31820222 Currently at: Front camber: -1.7 both sides Front toe: 0.05 both sides Rear Camber: -1.5 both sides Rear toe: 0.15 both sides Whenever a car is lowered or raised from what its ride height was engineered at, the amount of toe change when the wheel(s) move up and down will be greater than what it was originally designed for, which is what the driver percieves as bump steer. Really no way to fully correct roll center from lowering on the E9X platform besides custom knuckles. CMP Auto Engineering makes 8mm roll center compensation spacers for the front wishbone and front tie rod, which only correct roll center by 8mm out of the say 30 mm the car is lowered by. CMP's solution also neglects to correct the tension strut roll center since it can not be corrected with spacers, it would need to lower the ball joint on the knuckle or raise the bushing on the subframe end. I can't do the roll center and bump steer explanation justice without visuals, so here is a youtube link to a guy who IMO does a good explanation of it. There are a bunch of other videos on it as well.

body_text: i recently bought a beautiful E93 325i 2008 which i couldnt be happier with. the issue im having is im getting misfires just in cylinder 3 on cold starts and once the cars warmed up (10 - 20 mins later) it goes away. It also suffers from rough idle during this period too. I believe it to be the fuel injector as ive swapped the spark plugs and coils and that made a slight difference to feel but didnt fix misfire issue. im also leaking some oil so believe the valve cover could be the issue too but as im getting o2 sensor and mixture control 2 and 3 codes im thinking its the fuel injectors but i could be wrong. does anyone have any insights into this or experience with rough idle and fixes, im not super wealthy so open to DIY fixes and as ive just bought the car good things to look out for and fix. apologies if this isnt how to do forums or such, its my first time and would love to hear some peoples experiences or insights into what this could be or good things to look at to further diagnose the issue attached is the codes from Bimmerlink is there anything else that should be a concern like Ebox fan?? Many Thanks Thikboy

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: You will have to remove the rear glass at the very minimum. The front one can be retracted back a little so that it drops below the seal. Once the whole seal is accessible, just peel it off, clean the old glue residue, and apply new seal (locate the seam in the back, just like the original). Once you reinstall the rear glass, you will have to reset the sunroof operation as it needs to get adjusted to the new thicker seal.

raw_payload.author: mad1stgen

metadata_json.author: mad1stgen

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: I replaced the seal on my car without removing anything. With a combination of tilting and sliding the glass, remove the old rubber and clean off all the glue left behind. This is the method I found easiest for fitting the new seal with the glass in place. I peeled back about 20mm of the sticky tape and stuck it somewhere you can get at when the roof is tilted (at the back in the middle).  Once I had the back edge stuck on, with the seal on the outside of the car, I closed the roof.  With the roof closed I pushed the seal in to the gap (with the backing on the new seal it will slip in nice and easy).  With one hand I held the seal in place, and with the other I pulled the backing from the back of the new seal - it just slips out and as it does so it sticks the new seal to the body of the car. Just make sure that as you peel the backing away, the seal stays in the gap with your other hand.

body_text: Thanks so much for response! I would clarify as I got it at all.... Is just pull up for old seal removal right? Hi StuyMac, refer to your procedure as mentioned, the 20mm of sticky tape is sticking with back edge (the red color position of pic attached) of roof and new seal right? What about the side edge  ( left n right ) of back roof? I meant the blue area position, it's fixed position, how to peel the old one and install the new one? [IMG][/IMG]

metadata_json.subforum_name: General E90 / E91 / E92 / E93 Discussion
metadata_json.author: poseidonhkg

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: Yes, you just pull the old seal off. With the old seal out of the way you have a few mm gap which is big enough to get in a soft plastic instrument to remove the glue. With the roof tilted you can access the area marked in red.  Peel about 20mm of backing tape off the new seal and with the roof tilted start at the red marked area and work outwards.  With 1/2 the rear section done, close the roof.  Sticking out will be the rest of the new seal and the backing strip you started to peel. Tuck the new seal, still with backing in place, in the gap, making sure its flush.  Then, holding the seal in position with one hand, pull the backing upwards, away from the car.  As the backing comes off the seal it exposes the new glue, and as long as you hold the new seal in position, sticks it in place.  Do this with the roof closed until you get all the way round and back to the red mark where you started. Then tilt the roof again, trim the seal and join the ends

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by StuyMac Yes, you just pull the old seal off. With the old seal out of the way you have a few mm gap which is big enough to get in a soft plastic instrument to remove the glue. With the roof tilted you can access the area marked in red.  Peel about 20mm of backing tape off the new seal and with the roof tilted start at the red marked area and work outwards.  With 1/2 the rear section done, close the roof.  Sticking out will be the rest of the new seal and the backing strip you started to peel. Tuck the new seal, still with backing in place, in the gap, making sure its flush.  Then, holding the seal in position with one hand, pull the backing upwards, away from the car.  As the backing comes off the seal it exposes the new glue, and as long as you hold the new seal in position, sticks it in place.  Do this with the roof closed until you get all the way round and back to the red mark where you started. Then tilt the roof again, trim the seal and join the ends Hi StuyMac, thx so much I got it now Btw, did u change the REAR SLIDING/LIFTING ROOF COVER GASKET (part # 54137127565) as well please? Is eligible to replace the rear cover gasket during the surround seal please?

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: Update to this thread. Completed tonight in about an hour. Followed StuyMac 's procedure and it went much easier than anticipated. Couple things to add: - If you pull the old seal back on itself while removing @ a 180-degree angle I had a lot of luck with the glue coming off attached to the seal and not the car. Only had to remove about 2' in the rear section. When I pulled it @ a 90-degree angle it stuck to the car. - Followed the initialization procedure found here: I did have one issue. Right after I installed the seal the sunroof did not want to close and stay shut. It would automatically pop back up in the vent position. Almost as if it were too tight. I held the lock button from the outside (which shuts the windows and roof on my car) and gave some light pressure to the trailing edges of the glass. This got the roof to stay closed and then I was able to follow the initialization procedure. Thanks for the help, all!

title: Surround seal replacement for panoramic roof
body_text: Add me to the list of happy seal swappers! No noise at highway speed with the blind open! Amazing to get some extra light in during winter. It wasn’t possible for me to get the glue off with the seal. I had to rub at it with my finger after heating with hair dryer. Its not possible to get your finger in there with the rear glass in. I took it out and used the marks the gnurled washers made in the frame to get it lined back up. Its not obvious where the front screws are located at first. Mine we’re a bit rusted so be careful not to round the head. Don’t be tempted to cut the seal ‘long’ and have it compress together at the join. It just won’t sit right. Once I trimmed it a bit it came together really flush. Use a fresh razor blade to cut it square. I think scissors don’t do the best job. With the engine running hold the Vent button for 5 secs to initiate the initialisation process and the roof will cycle through Enjoy my pics, which this thread definitely lacked!

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.
body_text: Hello this is a post I was hoping to never make. I own a 2007 323i n52b25 with 337,446 km. This is not the first oil change were I’ve noticed this material in the oil filter but it’s the worst one yet for amount of material. The last 20 oil changes have all been done around 4000km apart and I would say this material only began to show up 8-10 or so changes ago. Or at least that’s when I noticed it. I will say I did use a liqui moly engine flush like 6 oil changes ago. Which I found to have basically no effect. It’s non ferris and brakes up pretty easy if you take some of it on you finger and wipe it on a blue shop towel it kind of just turns to dust or smaller parts. It breaks apart easily. It to my eye doesn’t appear to be metal it’s at least not shiny anyway. I also checked the Vanos filters after this oil change and they were spotless maybe 2 specs of material on them and I really had to look to find that. otherwise they were completely clean. The car runs flawlessly, starts great, runs great has good power, it doesn’t even have the Notorious N52 lifter tick the engine is completely silent. Like runs flawlessly. It burns roughly a liter of oil every 4000 ish km’s which is when I do oil changes anyway. Which imo for a N52B25 that seams quite good to me. Car is mostly spiritedly highway driven. I’m not sure what it could be. But it’s obviously not super critical yet. If it was metal you would think you’d see wear on the cam visible through the oil fill cap but it look exactly like it has for the past 5 years. (I check that came lobe frequently for some reason) Not sure if anyone has experienced this before or could advise me on what’s going on but I though I would at least make a post showing it. Some of the material to me almost seams like flakes of synthetic oil staining/light varnish that’s broken up not sure.

I’m using Mobil 1 0W40 charging every 10,000km with no drop in oil level between changes (no burning) It definitely could be some kind of coating like Hakentt suggested. How clean is the inside of your engine when you look down the oil cap? Mine has the brown varnish from previous owner following the oil change intervals from the on board computer. I have another n52 car that has no varnishing that I use the same mobil1 oil in. It’s due its first oil change in my ownership. I’ll report back if it also has the grey flakes. Blackstone labs can analyse an oil filter element but it’s expensive I think.

We literally see just about every possible failure these engines have seen, yet not a single mention of the failure you describe. Where did I mention that flaking is a failure? You are literally making stuff up. I owned you in this thread and I have proven you have no knowledge on N52 engine. Flaking starts later in engines life, like 15 years later then it progresses with age, most if is captured by the filter. BMW knew this will be the issue for magnesium block that is why they installed two filter capsules for vanos oil delivery plus they designed two vanos solenoids with fine mesh screens. Older BMW engines like M50 and M62 have magnesium valve covers and some of them flake so bad from the inside and outside that they require replacement. This means it will flake off to the point where the block is not good anymore. Aluminum and Iron do not flake off with time, this is why I said N52 is junk, it is something that can't be machined and rebuilt, it is a one use thing. Then explain to me why marketplace is full of 2006-2011 BMWs with N52 powered engines going for $1000 now, all mostly do to not running bad N52 engines.

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.  - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by lookalikehuuh You literally said that flaking magnesium makes the N52 junk.   Here's the interesting part, N54 and N55 engines, which have no magnesium in the blocks still utilize the same dual filter capsules fo the VANOS systems how do you explain that? Are iron blocks just junk too? How do you explain that in the S54 BMW also includes filters for the VANOS system? Is that all aluminum engine also having magnesium failures? How do you explain the N52s with 400Kmiles+ if they are junk? Also keep in mind that cars with N54 and N55 engines are actually cheaper than cars with N52 engines in the used market, its not because the engines are junk, it's because the vast majority of people don't maintain their cars, don't have the ability to fix it themselves and don't understand the more complex nature of the electronic systems in these cars.  This holds true for the vast majority of german cars, with a few exceptions.  Audi's are a dime a dozen for cheap everywhere, all generations of BMW (including ones without any magnesium parts whatsoever). Mercedes with everything from an inline 4 to a V12... Your logic... well has no logic behind it. Exactly. Many of us have N52 that have reached very high mileages of 300,000 and 400,000 miles and this guy comes in some 20 years after release of the mag-block N52 and says it is junk.  I just can't laugh hard enough.

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.  - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by lookalikehuuh That's just old oil varnish, probably from somewhat extended oil change intervals.  Nothing to worry about, just ensure you are changing your oil on time moving forward with good quality oils. I totally agree just varnish from extended oil change intervals. The previous owner just changed oil when the computer told him so highly extended. Under my ownership it’s never seen 5000km on the same oil. But interestingly this “material” in the filter has only started under my ownership. But still the car runs great so I guess it is what it is for now.

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.  - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by Lochstar8 I totally agree just varnish from extended oil change intervals. The previous owner just changed oil when the computer told him so highly extended. Under my ownership it’s never seen 5000km on the same oil. But interestingly this “material” in the filter has only started under my ownership. But still the car runs great so I guess it is what it is for now. OP, I'm not trying to beat a dead horse here, but in my opinion, you change your engine's oil way too early.  4,000km OCI is seriously way overkill.  That may be the problem.  What you are seeing may be the oil additive package chemicals not completely dissolving in the oil matrix.  You can research and find that changing modern oils too early is worse than letting the oil get used until the additives have been spent.  The problem with cartridge oil filters is the owner can see what the filter filters.  The filter is supposed to catch stuff in the oil.  What you are seeing may be completely normal for oil that is still new.  Below is the oil change data for my N52 that went 426,800 miles before a flood event this past May 2025 totaled my E90.  At that age the engine was consuming 1L every 2,500 miles or so and still was returning my lifetime fuel consumption average of 27 MPG (i.e. the N52 was running perfectly fine).  BMW enthusiasts way over analyze their oil choices and change schedule. In my opinion, using the correct oil filter is more important than the brand of oil (as long as it meets BMW's LL-01 spec and is the correct recommended viscosity). My 2 cents.

body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by Efthreeoh OP, I'm not trying to beat a dead horse here, but in my opinion, you change your engine's oil way too early.  4,000km OCI is seriously way overkill.  That may be the problem.  What you are seeing may be the oil additive package chemicals not completely dissolving in the oil matrix.  You can research and find that changing modern oils too early is worse than letting the oil get used until the additives have been spent.  The problem with cartridge oil filters is the owner can see what the filter filters.  The filter is supposed to catch stuff in the oil.  What you are seeing may be completely normal for oil that is still new.  Below is the oil change data for my N52 that went 426,800 miles before a flood event this past May 2025 totaled my E90.  At that age the engine was consuming 1L every 2,500 miles or so and still was returning my lifetime fuel consumption average of 27 MPG (i.e. the N52 was running perfectly fine).  BMW enthusiasts way over analyze their oil choices and change schedule. In my opinion, using the correct oil filter is more important than the brand of oil (as long as it meets BMW's LL-01 spec and is the correct recommended viscosity). My 2 cents. Attachment 3855447 That’s certainly a possibility as oil changes were longer prior to my ownership and this really only started happening during my ownership. I use a MANN HU816X oil filter which is what the car has had its entire life. As well as the LL-01 full synthetic castrol oil it’s entire life. So I guess the only real variable surrounding what I’ve done regarding oil is change frequency. One of the main reasons I’ve been changing it more frequently is because around 4500km on an oil change seems to be the exact mileage the engine gets noticeably noisier ie: more of that glorious n52 lifter tick. Despite my driving habits not changing. Any mileage under that, or with fresh oil the car never ticks even on startup it’s genuinely one of the quietest n52s I’ve ever heard except my failing ac compressor or wheel bearings but that’s besides the point. I could try going back to a 10k km engine oil changes and see if that makes any difference it may take a few changes to take affect. I really don’t want to go much higher than that. I drive this car relatively hard especially in the snow covered months possibly even ice racing on the very odd occasion. I’ll try an oil change at 10k for the next 2-3 changes and report back if I remember.(If I don’t get a new car buy then) which I probably won’t because BMW themselves barley makes a straight 6 RWD manual these days at least not one I can afford anyway. Maybe for this first change at 5k I’ll throw a new filter in it but leave the old oil till 10k that way I can not only check up on the material but also make damn sure it doesn’t completely clog the filter. I get MANN HU816X’s at a pretty hefty discount so I don’t mind swapping filters often. Plus that will give me an idea if it gets better or worse during the first or second half of the oils life span. Thanks for the input.

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.  - Page 2
body_text: Lochstar8 You didn't mention if you have a short commute or not. If you're not letting it get up to operating temp for extended period (20min?), it could be keeping moisture in the engine creating sludge and making some weird mixture that your filter is correctly pulling out. Anyway, maybe not the problem at all, just throwing it out there. But wanted to emphasize the filter is doing its job as intended!

But little drifting solves that pretty quick. So in my case I don’t think that’s my problem. But a very valid concern nonetheless.

I use a MANN HU816X oil filter which is what the car has had its entire life. As well as the LL-01 full synthetic castrol oil it’s entire life. So I guess the only real variable surrounding what I’ve done regarding oil is change frequency. One of the main reasons I’ve been changing it more frequently is because around 4500km on an oil change seems to be the exact mileage the engine gets noticeably noisier ie: more of that glorious n52 lifter tick. Despite my driving habits not changing. Any mileage under that, or with fresh oil the car never ticks even on startup it’s genuinely one of the quietest n52s I’ve ever heard except my failing ac compressor or wheel bearings but that’s besides the point. I could try going back to a 10k km engine oil changes and see if that makes any difference it may take a few changes to take affect. I really don’t want to go much higher than that. I drive this car relatively hard especially in the snow covered months possibly even ice racing on the very odd occasion. I’ll try an oil change at 10k for the next 2-3 changes and report back if I remember.(If I don’t get a new car buy then) which I probably won’t because BMW themselves barley makes a straight 6 RWD manual these days at least not one I can afford anyway. Maybe for this first change at 5k I’ll throw a new filter in it but leave the old oil till 10k that way I can not only check up on the material but also make damn sure it doesn’t completely clog the filter. I get MANN HU816X’s at a pretty hefty discount so I don’t mind swapping filters often. Plus that will give me an idea if it gets better or worse during the first or second half of the oils life span. Thanks for the input. Good on the MANN filter, that is all my N52 in the E90 ever had in it as well.  Also, I used only BMW's oil LL-01 5W-30. I know it is difficult for people to break the habit of early oil changes, but I've proven what seems to be ridiculously long drain intervals are quite tolerable with the N52.  Believe me, I was skeptical at first, and had serious pucker factor when the first change went 17,000 miles and worst, the engine did not consume any oil until it needed a quart a few weeks before the OCI notification came up (I checked it twice - @ change #3 and #14 posted above).  I did not trust the oil quality sensor (OZS), but the E90 when new was under BMW's pre-paid (cough-cough - free) maintenance plan. I drove my E90 very, very hard day in and day out on the excellent back-country roads we have here in my local in the Virginia Blueridge Mountains.  Just a mile from the house is an 11-turn corkscrew that drives up over a ridge, 800 feet elevation change in just 1 mile of roadway.  I took that road every day for 15 years on a cold engine.  And then hammered down it on the way home.  A true test of the N52 lubrication system to not scavenge on high-G turns.  My car also saw a lot of heavy stop-and-go traffic too on my commute.  The one good attribute of my commute was the heat cycling was limited; the engine would see a good 1 hour and 45 minutes between cool down periods.  There is no doubt the long heat cycle periods helped in achieving the high mileage the engine reached.  But once the engine is hot, it is hot. 10 klicks on the OCI may help improve what you (won't) see in the filter.

title: Material In N52 Oil Filter.  - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by Hakentt that is protective coating chipping away from magnesium block, all N52 have that issue, that is why N52 is junk Fascinating. This lead me down the rabbit hole of magnesium alloys and their coatings. I wonder what conditions lead to this issue? Excess moisture in the oil? Oil becomes too acidic? Or does the coating just fail overtime? I doubt many people would even realize it was happening unless they inspect their filter media closely (even I don't). I don't think OP is looking at oil varnish as that is typically much darker in color. I looked at a few used blocks on eBay and didn't see any with peeling coatings on the bedplate. However, I did see numerous reports of the magnesium bedplate developing cracks and the leading theory is due to inconsistent thermal expansion rates. Might be more of an issue on track cars driven hard. Very interested to learn more. The N52 does feel like BMW's beta test of some technologies, not all of which worked out.

Thanks for the input. Mileage alone is insufficient for determining oil change intervals. It greatly depends on your operating conditions. If you do city driving or short trips with more start ups and heat cycles then I would change the oil on a time based interval. Oil is cheap and easy to DIY; you don't really gain anything by extended intervals, especially with the amount of debris you're seeing in the filter.

title: ELV/CAS No Start
body_text: Hello, I have a 2006 bmw 330xi manual that won’t start or even crank. The screen shows a yellow steering wheel lock and the ignition doesn’t turn on. I installed a elv emulator but I hasn’t worked. I have used all of the different standard tools to try and reset or delete fault codes and elv counters. I replaced the battery because I thought it might be a voltage issue and that didn’t work either. Does anyone have any ideas what I can do or trouble shooting I can do? Thanks!

title: ELV/CAS No Start
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 ... I have bmw standard tools on my laptop... Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 Both fuses f4 and f50 tested good. On a 2006 E90, F51 is the "Load" fuse. That's the one to check. If that is OK, then use INPA, BMW Factory-Level diagnostic software, to assess CAS operation. See the attached INPA ScreenPrint, showing the Status of CAS Terminals. Also see the attached pdf, INPA Tutorial Quickstart, for how to use INPA. The path, or Menu Sequence, to view that INPA Screen: INPA > (Body | Car Access System) > F5 Status > F2 Analog > F1 Diagnose Terminals If you will view, save & Post a ScreenPrint of that INPA Screen at each of these Ignition/Key positions, we can suggest "Next Steps: 1) Ignition ON; 2) Ignition OFF, but Remote Key still inserted; 3) Ignition ON; Brake Pressed along with START button (what you would press to Crank Starter); The attached INPA ScreenPrints from my properly-functioning 328xi show: a) Ignition OFF, immediately after Ignition ON; b) Ignition ON; c) START & Brake Pressed (Start & KL50 Active). I've shown you mine; please show us YOURS George

title: ELV/CAS No Start
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 I have a 2006 bmw 330xi manual that won’t start or even crank. The screen shows a yellow steering wheel lock and the ignition doesn’t turn on. I installed a elv emulator but it hasn’t worked... Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 I’m getting codes A0B2 and A0B5 Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 My ignition won’t even turn on Perhaps I missed it, but I'm not sure from your posts to date whether the vehicle has EVER started during your ownership, or if so, WHEN the "No Crank" condition 1st occurred, or WHAT events (work/changes on vehicle) preceded the "NO Crank". Those facts would aid DIAGNOSIS. If you have never used INPA, some explanation will speed your diagnosis: 1) CAS is powered by TWO fuses. On your 2006 model, those fuses are F4 & F51; 2) F4 is the 5-Amp "Electronics" fuse. Its voltage is shown on the INPA "Terminals" screen (mine was attached to post #14) as "KL30E", on Right of Top Row of bar graphs. If CAS were NOT getting power from that fuse, due to bad fuse or line fault, you would NOT be able to connect to CAS Module. 'KL' is German abbreviation for "Terminal" (Klemme, which can also mean 'clamp'). Folks who attempted translation from German to English chose "Ignition" in error. Google Translate, if used correctly, will give reasonable translations from technical German to Technical English: https://translate.google.com/?sl=de&...e&op=translate 3) F51 is the 50-Amp "Load" fuse. It's voltage is shown on INPA "Terminals" screen, on Left of Top Row, as "KL30L". THAT is the first thing to check in your case. ISTA ScreenPrint showing location of F51 on 2006 Fuse Panel is attached. While a fault in CAS Module is possible, a fuse, wiring/line or Connector issue is more-statistically-likely. 4) You probably cannot connect to the CAS Module unless the Ignition is ON (START button pressed WITHOUT pressing Brake/Clutch). You say: "The screen shows a yellow steering wheel lock and the ignition doesn’t turn on." I don't have a "Nav" or CID Screen so am confused as to what 'Screen' you are referencing, or what does NOT happen when you attempt to turn ignition ON. On my 3/2007 build 328xi, when I insert Remote Key, press START button, WITHOUT pressing Brake/Clutch, the Instrument Cluster lights & "Ignition is ON". The INPA 'Terminals' screen shows KL15 ON. Terminal 15 is Ignition. There are 3 KL15 Bar Graphs: KL15-1, KL15-2 & KL15-3. There is also a KL15  Wakeup (WUP) Bar Graph. 5) KLR is the Terminal that SHOULD be activated by simply inserting the Remote Key in the Insert Compartment. When KLR is active, the Radio plays, the Brake lights operate, & the MRS module is powered. 6) KL30G is the Terminal that activates the accessory Relay, which is the large, black, removable relay on the right of the JB Fuse panel. When that relay is activated, Accessories, such as Climate Control Panel are powered. The panel lights & the blower motor functions. KL30G remains active for several minutes AFTER Ignition OFF, at least if Remote Key is NOT removed. 7) KL50 is the CAS Terminal that sends voltage to the Starter Solenoid, to Crank/Activate the Starter. If that signal is sent, but there is NO engine RPM per Crank Sensor signal to DME, an "A0B4" fault code is normally saved in CAS. You do NOT report that code. 8) You report 2 CAS faults, A0B2 & A0B5; BMW Fault Code Lookup provides the following definitions: A0B2| CAS: Supply, terminal 30E/30L | cas | Car Access System A0B5 | CAS: Fault, road speed signal | cas | Car Access System https://bmwfault.codes/Lookup There are other INPA Screens which can show you Signals received by CAS: a) when Remote Key is inserted/removed; b) when Brake Pedal is pressed (via Brake Light Switch); c) when Clutch Pedal is pressed (via Clutch Switch); d) when AT (if present) gear is "P" or "N". If any of those required signals are not received, Starter may not crank. My 3/2007 build 328xi has the later EWS4 & NO Steering Lock, so I have NOT viewed INPA screens related to those two devices. While you MAY have an issue with the steering lock itself, the A0B2 fault appears to indicate a more fundamental issue/fault. I would encourage you to take the time to learn how to use INPA to understand exactly what signals your CAS module is or is NOT receiving. George

title: ELV/CAS No Start
body_text: Check your battery well and see if it's flooded with water. If no water, check the two tables at the bottom of them well for any corrosions. Check the fuses in power distribution unit (the red module on top of battery). I had water flooded in the battery well for awhile and it corroded these two terminals. Both cables were completely severed. See link for photo: https://www.e90post.com/forums/showthread.php?t=1803532 If everything checks out ok with no corrosion, then your CAS or DME could be toasted. It took me 4 months to troubleshoot CAS code A103 which wasn't the CAS module at all. It was the DME that failed due to water in the battery well.

title: ELV/CAS No Start - Page 2
body_text: I got my car running!!!! The connector between the ELV and the CAS was broken, one of the pins was actually broken inside and not making contact, I cut and crimped the one broken wire with my own connector and ignition turned on and started the car. My battery died while sitting overnight but that’s a different problem…

title: ELV/CAS No Start - Page 2
body_text: Quote: Originally Posted by treycullison57 I got my car running!!!! The connector between the ELV and the CAS was broken, one of the pins was actually broken inside and not making contact, I cut and crimped the one broken wire with my own connector and ignition turned on and started the car... Glad you found the cause of the issue. General Rule of Diagnostics: "Visually inspect & electrically TEST (Multimeter) all wiring & connectors FIRST." If you do NOT have Bentley Manual, or ISTA, which contain the wiring diagrams, or need help testing, please ASK. Most folks want to replace a component (BEFORE Testing), 'cuz that's what someone else claims fixed their similar issue. We've ALL "feared the worst/most expensive". So folks can see the circuit, & the location & connector view of the intermediate connector, X10254 (I presume that's where you say the issue was), I attach ISTA ScreenPrints of all wiring & connectors involved, from the CAS Module to the ELV (M137a). George

title: Tariffs and duties....BS
body_text: Rant - but also PSA for people thinking about buying from Europe. Back in November I ordered a few parts of ebay out of Europe - sill trim and tail light lenses. Didn't have any added fees at the time. Out of the blue last week, I get notices from FedEx to pay up. Problem is, they have the duties wrong. It's a f-ing racket. On two of the invoices they have applied duties that are mutually exclusive, one is newer than the other and if they apply the new one the old one should be left off. Then they have another that is just totally wrong - aimed and heavy equipment and trucks. On one of these, the total duties are near 50% of the cost of the parts. To be clear, DHS imposes the duties, FedEx paid them and are now seeking reimbursement. So it's DHSs s#itty, corrupt adjusters that start all this. I disputed it with FedEx, I think it's the only recourse. Their response is because the total is less than $200, they impart a $90-150 fee to process disputes from a customer as opposed to FedEx disputing it. Who the F else is going to dispute duties? FedEx has no idea what the parts are or what the intended use is. It's such a huge racket. So glad we are still wining.

title: E92 330i Charging Fault
body_text: Hi guys, I just bought an E92 330. The charging system fault light came on twice on the very first day. The warnings became more frequent the more I drove it — at worst, it showed the fault seven times during a roughly 10‑km traffic jam. Last Sunday the warning stayed on nonstop until the battery went completely dead. I had the alternator repaired, and the internal voltage regulator was replaced. It could output 14V on the test bench. At the same time, I also replaced the electric water pump, alternator belt, and tensioner. But the fault still appears. Unplugging the alternator connector restores normal charging, but I’m worried the battery will be overcharged and damaged. The warning pops up under all driving conditions, and turning off the air conditioning didn’t help at all. The mechanic who repaired the alternator said it’s not the alternator that’s faulty, but a signal problem, and told me to check the ignition coils, water pump, AC condenser and engine oil level sensor. I replaced the ignition coils with Delphi ones a few days ago, and spark plugs with NGK at the same time. The water pump is a Dolz unit. The oil level sensor hasn’t been replaced yet, and it’s leaking oil now. The belt and tensioner are Gates brand. The battery is still the original Varta standard 82Ah lead‑acid, not AGM. After fully charging it with a charger during repair, the multimeter showed 12.4V. I just performed the battery registration procedure, but it made no difference. Now, when the engine is warm at idle, the voltage shown via the diagnostic computer is about 12.7V–13V. The car shows no problem about getting started. AC works fine too, though not blasting cold. Do you have any suggestions on what to check next? Wiring harnesses? Oil level sensor? AC condenser ? Or should I just replace the battery directly? Thanks in advance for all your helpful information and kind replies.

title: m54b30 swap for my e92
body_text: I'm looking for some advice on my E92 330i.The engine (N52B30) has misfires on cylinder 1 and 2 with coolant in the cylinders. I've decided not to repair it and want to swap in an M54B30 instead.My current transmission is a GS6-17BG from a 328i. Mechanically, the swap looks pretty straightforward.I know I'll need the E60 E-box harness and an MS45 DME. However, I'm not sure how to handle the wiring for the A/C system and the other ECU components.Please don't try to talk me out of it — I just really like the M54.Has anyone done this exact swap (M54 into E92) before? If so, could you share any wiring diagrams or tips on integrating the M54 with the E92’s electronics, especially the A/C and other ECUs?Thanks in advance for any help or shared experience!

title: m54b30 swap for my e92
body_text: If you do this swap you will run into CAN bus issues. The CAN bus system in an E9x is different to an E6x, so you will have trouble getting the E6x DME to communicate with the likes of the instrument cluster, DSC, JBE and FRM.

title: 2007 328i Leather Seat "Scuff" Mark
body_text: Hello, getting our 328i ready for spring.  The front passenger seat has a black "scuff" mark that I have not been able to remove with Lexol or Zymol cleaner and soft cloth.  The seats are in generally excellent condition, but could also use advice about products/techniques to get into the cracks/pores of the seats.  Thanks very much.

title: 2007 328i Leather Seat "Scuff" Mark
body_text: Hi, I tried more cleaning, and determined that the streak is actually the top color scuffed through to the underlying base leather I think. I've been in contact with Leatherworld tech support, and it looks like their BMW Cream Beige is the correct match for my seat color based on VIN. Now - I also want to fix up some trim pieces which are scratched and worn like the bezel cover on the rear seat tray (see pic). From a lot of searching I think the correct Color Bond spray is 657 Savannah Beige.  It could possibly be Color Bond 1870 Cream Beige (lighter). My problem is that I am in Canada and they do not ship outside the US.  Does anyone know of a source within Canada that has colors to match the 657 and/or the 1870? There are a few sources that will ship to Canada from the US, but shipping right now is very expensive - like US$55 to ship US$25 in spray paint.  Any potential options in model paint for example that would work well on automotive plastics?? Thanks for ideas/experience with similar issue.

title: 2007 328i Leather Seat "Scuff" Mark
body_text: If you saturate the leather (with conditioner) and the scuff turns dark black, then the scuff is through to the hide of the leather and the area will need to be re-dyed. (At that point I'd try to find a ColourLock repairer). If not, and it's just a rubber (or shoe) scuff, I wouldn't be too aggressive with scrubbing it otherwise you might wear the leather dye off in the area. Best to be patient and use a proper leather product to "lift" the dirt so it can be wiped away. I use Leatherique, with great results on all my cars, it's very time consuming (days to weeks sometimes) but worth it to me for the results I'm looking for.

title: Fixing a free e90
body_text: I have a 2010 328i e90 automatic LHD that was given to me for free. Doing some research on the VIN i can see it was a crash damaged, manual e90 from Africa. I've been going through the problems with it so far with some of them being a fried FRM module, a lot of cut wires up at the front of the car on the headlight harness and misc pieces missing. The car starts and the engine sounds healthy, but I've been met with transmission codes which put the car into a limp mode whenever you try to put it into gear. Codes associated with this being  581A, 581B, 581C, 581D and 5658. All speed sensor related. I've already done a service on the transmission to rule that out as a possibility. So with that being said. I recently went on a trip and forgot to disconnect the battery on the car. When I got back the battery was dead. I recharged it and went back into trying to diagnose it and realized none of the ECU's were showing up. The only thing that sort of communicated was the JBBF module. I connected with INPA and saw the JBBF had basically lost the vin that was coded to it, and everything else. Weirdly enough, the car still starts, but shifting does nothing. Car doesnt respond to throttle. Which i understand is because the JBBF/JBE is in charge of waking up the other modules. So what I'm wondering is if the JBBF is recoverable by any means. I've tried coding it through Winkfp and only being met with errors. I've tried multiple sp-datens and am somewhat familiar with the process of using bmw tools. I've updated the cas on my 2008 e91 recently to try to fix an ELV issue. So is this a situation that can be fixed with coding, or do I need a new JBBF/JBE. Thank you!
